messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":38,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"mechanic","city_of_living":"Milan","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["classical music","hiking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":48,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":75,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":24,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":50,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

  "opinion": "As a mechanic in Milan, I approach fake content on social media with the same practical, hands-on mindset I use in my workshop. I don't spend much time online—just a few times a month—so when I do, I want it to be straightforward and reliable, not cluttered with nonsense. My vocational training taught me to rely on evidence and clear procedures, not hearsay. For instance, if I see a post claiming a car part is dangerous, I check official recalls or trusted automotive forums before believing it, just as I'd verify a tool's specs before using it. I'm naturally skeptical due to my low agreeableness, so I don't just accept what others share; I question sources and look for inconsistencies. In my social circles, which are more about hiking and classical music than online debates, I encourage friends to do the same—think critically and avoid spreading unverified info. Platforms should make it easier to report fake content, but ultimately, it's on users to be vigilant. I don't get worked up over it, thanks to my moderate neuroticism and good mental health, but I see it as a nuisance that wastes time and can mislead people. By staying informed through reputable sources and not engaging with suspicious posts, I keep my online experience clean and focused, much like maintaining a well-organized toolbox.",

High extraversion (score 75) indicates social engagement but limited online time (few times monthly) focuses on quality interactions. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a mechanic in Milan, I approach fake content on social media with the same practical, hands-on mindset I use in my workshop. I don't spend much time online—just a few times a month—so when I do, I want it to be straightforward and reliable, not cluttered with nonsense. My vocational training taught me to rely on evidence and clear procedures, not hearsay. For instance, if I see a post claiming a car part is dangerous, I check official recalls or trusted automotive forums before believing it, just as I'd verify a tool's specs before using it. I'm naturally skeptical due to my low agreeableness, so I don't just accept what others share; I question sources and look for inconsistencies. In my social circles, which are more about hiking and classical music than online debates, I encourage friends to do the same—think critically and avoid spreading unverified info. Platforms should make it easier to report fake content, but ultimately, it's on users to be vigilant. I don't get worked up over it, thanks to my moderate neuroticism and good mental health, but I see it as a nuisance that wastes time and can mislead people. By staying informed through reputable sources and not engaging with suspicious posts, I keep my online experience clean and focused, much like maintaining a well-organized toolbox.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 38 and occupation as mechanic suggest practical, experienced approach. Vocational diploma and parents' mixed education imply value in hands-on learning and critical thinking. Low agreeableness (score 24) leads to skepticism and independence in evaluating information. High extraversion (score 75) indicates social engagement but limited online time (few times monthly) focuses on quality interactions. Moderate openness (score 48) and conscientiousness (score 51) support balanced, methodical handling of fake content. Atheism and good mental health contribute to rational, unemotional perspective. Hobbies like hiking and classical music emphasize real-world interests over online drama. Examples tied to mechanical work (e.g., checking car part recalls) align with occupational background. Overall tone is direct, no-nonsense, and solution-oriented, avoiding bullet points as requested.
response_parsed.tone: direct, practical, skeptical, and no-nonsense, with a focus on real-world applications and minimal online engagement.
